activities.markdown:
# Bossa Nova

Bossa nova fuses Brazilian samba with jazz harmony. The hi-hat foot pattern is the most distinctive element — it plays a clave-inspired syncopated pattern while the snare provides a light brushed backbeat.

Bossa is played at medium tempo with a gentle, laid-back feel. Brushes are strongly preferred over sticks — they produce the soft, whispery texture that defines the style. The kick is syncopated, never aggressive.

Key records to study: João Gilberto's recordings, 'The Girl from Ipanema' (Astrud Gilberto), and any Antonio Carlos Jobim album.

activities.title: Bossa Nova Pattern
activities.description: The bossa nova groove: hi-hat plays the syncopated clave-inspired pattern, snare plays a soft brushed backbeat on 2 and 4, kick syncopates against both. The open feel between the downbeats is essential — resist filling every space.

activities.question: What is the primary feel difference between bossa nova and standard rock drumming?
choices: Bossa nova has a relaxed, syncopated feel based on the clave vs. rock's driving backbeat
Bossa nova uses only kick and snare
There is no difference in feel
activities.explanation: Bossa nova has a gentle, swaying, syncopated feel rooted in the Brazilian clave rhythm, very different from rock's aggressive backbeat drive.
